text: it. His eyes were wet and he’d lost his hat somewhere. “They got my son’s baby girl,” Lupe whispered. “I hear her fussing.” The rest of what he said collapsed into a sob and we watched him climb down from his horse and approach the mound. Not one of us spoke a word as Lupe bent over and slipped into the darkness of the opening. As if his movement had tipped the axle of the world itself, night began to fill the city. The three left in our procession looked straight up and there nearer than ever were the stars packed into a miasma. They looked an arm’s reach away, and I didn’t lower my eyes as William too crawled into the hole. “Gris, you coming?” Still I kept my eyes in the heavens. “Reckon so, Sorrén,” I said. It was awful to hear my own voice or anything but Isa singing of Adelín and all those vaqueros along the train of her dress. There were a great many other sounds and voices but they only served as the pulse of percussion. Then Sorrén was gone and I sat numb on my horse. The other animals folded themselves down to the earth. I ruminated on keyholes and the spaces between stars. Isa sang to me and I felt my blood pulling in my veins like iron filings to a magnet. In the last red streaks of sun I saw the blood man’s stone lying beside his horse. I dismounted and walked into the rising chorus of song—voices piling onto Isa’s now—and hefted it in my hand. Silence sheared through all and devastated me. I watched the naked men wriggling into the surface of the mound. I laid my other hand on the slick wall and felt something hum through every piece of me before that too was sucked up into the stone. My horse nudged me on the shoulder and I fell back casting my eyes around that dead city. A crowd of folk gathered in the dark street. Nude forms with distended bellies and their eyeless faces tilted up toward me. In the near distance I saw others scuttle to meet them, dragging their vulgar pregnancies in the dirt. I turned back in witness of a far greater arrival. Behind the mound a shadow swelled in the night. The earth trembled with the immense weight of something both walking and crawling toward the town. The clump of stars was blotted out as a vast black thing stood in front of them and unfurled itself. In my distraction the townsfolk had closed upon me. I pulled my blade and cut down something that might have once been the city governor. His hanging belly strained in a dying attempt at birth. The pale woman from the hearsewagon clasped her arms around my legs. Another of the nude figures grappled with my sword, its fingers coming off against the blade. Soon I was backed onto the narrow boardwalk before the cantina. The great shadow filled yet more of the sky and I sensed something reaching down toward me. I saw my horse in the distance and broke for it, pulling myself up into the saddle. “Isa love, forgive me,” I said with a look back at the mound, its mouth vague and muted by the blood man’s stone, which I still held inside my shirt against my skin. I blessed the horse for not abandoning me, then turned and rode north out of El Porvenir at a gallop, striking at those still in my path. # The mountains proved as hard to reach as the city had the day before. Their dim peaks were like old friends and though I foolishly rode through the night they seemed to draw little closer. The strange sounds of this new world remained muted so long as the stone touched my flesh. An hour before dawn the horse went down with a scream and though I fell and rolled with all my old ease, the stone was flung out of my shirt. It skittered across the parched hardpan and in an instant I knew I had been followed. My eldest daughter was in my ear. “It’s Mama’s time,” Benicia said. Her voice was cracked and the cracks were filled with sorrow. “She’s calling for you to come see her out of this life.” A low singing like a breeze. The land was starless and turned black as the throat of a mine. Then I heard Isa moaning somewhere behind me. I turned and could not tell if it was my wife or my horse in pain. On my knees I flailed, my arms reached out searching for the stone in all that impossible and coarse nothingness, a pebble in a blind wasteland. Breath fell across my face. Something combed through my hair and circled my wrists and I was pulled forward, the familiar old tune sighing above me. I called my wife’s name in a litany. By the grace of some faith I didn’t dare address the wizard’s stone smashed into my chin and lodged against my neck. My hands were released and deafness fell once more in a sharp hush over everything. I kissed that stone. I laid my head upon it and watched for the sun to break open the east. # The horse had snapped a foreleg and lay rolling its eyes. Its barrel was strangely bloated. I drove my sword into its brain and gathered what precious little gear I had, then setting my eyes upon the changed land I walked into the morning, kerchief tied to my face against the dervishes of sand. For another mound had appeared, to the north between me and the mountains, and a second rose perhaps seven miles to the west. I thought of Apache burial grounds, though they had always planted their dead in rows where tree roots could entwine their bones. Mounds were used by tribes in parts of the world I’d only heard
